cue: How do you say someone tells tall tales?

es.meaning: To come telling tall tales or excuses; to spin a line.
es.example: No me vengas con músicas, dime la verdad.
es.exampleTranslation: Don't give me that song and dance — tell me the truth.

it.meaning: To spin tales; to make excuses.
it.example: Non raccontarmi storie, dimmi la verità.
it.exampleTranslation: Don't tell me stories — tell me the truth.

bridge.note: Both dismiss a lie as a performance, and pick a different art form. Spanish hears MUSIC — venir con músicas, arriving with a whole tune to distract you, the con man as one-man band. Italian hears STORIES — raccontare storie, the plain fiction of it. Curiously here English lines up with Spanish, calling it a song and dance, while Italian stays flat. Note the near-false-friend: Italian 'storie' as excuses, not 'historias' as tales.

interference.es_to_it: 'Venire con musiche' is not idiomatic; Italian tells stories — raccontare storie. And storie here means baloney, not history.
interference.it_to_es: 'Contar historias' is weaker in Spanish; the dismissive idiom is venir con músicas.

review.notes: ES side discovered in the Spanish idioms book Joe is reading, 2026-07-21 (discovery only; all glosses and examples authored fresh). IT counterpart from Claude's recall — UNVERIFIED. Music-vs-story; English aligns with Spanish for once. The storie/historias false friend is a bonus.
